title: Do Androids Laugh at Electric Sheep? Humor “Understanding” Benchmarks from The New Yorker Caption Contest
research_questions: Can AI models recognize when a caption is appropriate for a given New Yorker cartoon?
Can AI models identify highly rated or finalist captions among captions written for the same cartoon?
Can AI models generate explanations of why a caption-and-image combination is funny that are as good as human-authored explanations?
How much of model performance is limited by visual recognition when comparing From Pixels and From Description settings?
How do fine-tuning, in-context learning, model scale, and GPT-4 affect matching, ranking, and explanation quality?

datasets_used.name: New Yorker Caption Contest corpus
datasets_used.size: 704 cartoons; 2.7K high quality captions; 651 human-created joke explanations; Matching train/val/test 1.6K / 538 / 538; Quality ranking train/val/test 1.6K / 523 / 523; Explanation train/val/test 391 / 130 / 130

datasets_used.name: Jain et al. (2020) New Yorker cartoon caption contest dataset
datasets_used.size: roughly 250 contests (mean/median 6.1K/5.7K unique captions per contest; 1.5M total); over 114M ratings total (mean/median of 445K/471K per contest)

datasets_used.name: Shahaf et al. (2015); Radev et al. (2016) corpus
datasets_used.size: contests #1–#507; 2M unique captions (mean/median 5.2K/5.0K per contest); 55 contests removed by hand; 80 low resolution but usable cases identified

methods_proposed.name: New Yorker Caption Contest humor-understanding benchmark suite
methods_proposed.summary: The paper proposes three progressively harder benchmark tasks derived from the caption contest: 5-way caption-cartoon matching, quality ranking between a finalist/high-quality caption and an “okay” caption, and free-text explanation generation.

methods_proposed.name: From Pixels and From Description evaluation settings
methods_proposed.summary: In the From Pixels setting, models receive only cartoon images at test time; in the From Description setting, models receive human-authored annotations of scene location, literal description, uncanny/unusual elements, and relevant Wikipedia entities to factor out visual recognition.

methods_proposed.name: OFA-Huge → language model pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: The paper fine-tunes OFA-Huge to generate the same annotation types from cartoon images, then passes those predicted descriptions to T5 models for downstream multiple-choice and explanation tasks.

humor_theories.detail: The benchmark annotations include descriptions of what is unusual or uncanny in each cartoon, and the related-work discussion notes that most New Yorker caption contest cartoons involve incongruous situations.
The related-work section summarizes Attardo and Raskin’s “hostility” family of humor theories as claims of superiority over someone or something.
The related-work section lists “release” of a constraint as one of the three major families of theories of humor.

evaluation_methods.detail: Matching and quality ranking are evaluated with accuracy averaged over 5 cross-validation splits; quality ranking reports CrowdAcc and NYAcc.
Three people each attempted 100 randomly sampled instances from both matching and quality ranking in an image-only setting; 595 total responses were collected.
For explanation, 3 crowdworkers judged each pair of explanations, and a majority vote determined the winner; Table 3 reports % A wins, # ratings, and Gwet’s γ.
Appendix E reports BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, and word-level perplexity for explanation generation, while noting that human evaluations are better quality measures.
Crowd ratings from the NEXT platform, where readers rate captions as “funny”, “somewhat funny”, or “unfunny”, are used to form crowd-ranked captions and CrowdAcc targets.
One author wrote 651 reference joke explanations after filtering out cases where the author did not understand the joke.

headline_results.finding: In the From Pixels setting, the best matching model was far below the human estimate.
In the From Description setting, GPT-4 with 5 in-context examples achieved the best matching accuracy.
For quality ranking against crowd preferences, GPT-4 with 5 in-context examples was best but remained below the human estimate.
For quality ranking against New Yorker editor selections, fine-tuned GPT-3 was best and exceeded the human estimate.
Human-authored explanations were preferred over the best machine-authored explanations, GPT-4 with 5 in-context examples.
headline_results.metric: % A wins in pairwise human evaluation

headline_results.finding: With equal amounts of supervision, GPT-4 explanations were preferred over GPT-3 explanations nearly universally.
headline_results.metric: % A wins in pairwise human evaluation

key_findings: The best From Pixels matching model, fine-tuned CLIP ViT-L/14@336px, achieved 62.3 accuracy, while the human estimate was 94.0.
From Description models performed substantially better on matching, with GPT-4 (5-shot) reaching 84.5 accuracy.

For quality ranking, fine-tuned GPT-3 achieved the best NYAcc at 69.8, exceeding the human estimate of 64.6, while GPT-4 (5-shot) achieved the best CrowdAcc at 73.3 but trailed the human estimate of 83.7.
Explanation generation strongly benefited from image context: T5-11B beat the caption-only version in 84.7% of pairwise cases.
Computer vision remained a bottleneck for explanations: From Description T5-11B was preferred over OFA → T5-11B in 74.6% of cases.
Human explanations were still preferred over 5-shot GPT-4 explanations in 67.7% of pairwise cases.
limitations: The New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest is a narrow slice of humor tied to a particular language, region, history, culture, style, and set of conventions.
Quality ranking may look prescriptive, but the authors state that New Yorker editorial selections should not be treated as objective ground truth for funniness.
The tasks operationalize average preferences rather than individual tastes and may reflect bias toward characteristics of prior contest winners or published New Yorker cartoons.
The explanation corpus was largely written by a single author, so future work should compare person-to-person variance in joke explanations.
The data may contain offensive jokes, although the authors manually removed a handful of cases observed to target specific protected classes.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that the data may contain offensive jokes, says the authors manually removed a handful of cases targeting specific protected classes, and states that they do not endorse the jokes. Crowdworkers were paid a minimum of $15/hr, adult-content warnings were shown for relevant HITs, worker IDs were hashed in the public release, and the authors report that their IRB considered the work exempt.

artifacts.other: The paper says it releases models, a leaderboard, annotations, and corpus at https://capcon.dev/.

human_samples.population: New Yorker cartoonists whose captionless cartoons appeared in the contest corpus
human_samples.recruitment: Cartoons were drawn from 14 years of weekly New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contests and prior released corpora.
human_samples.observations: 704 cartoons used in the benchmark after filtering; the paper does not report the number of distinct cartoonists.
human_samples.detail: Cartoons supply the visual stimuli for matching, quality ranking, explanation, and annotation tasks.

human_samples.population: New Yorker Caption Contest entrants/readers submitting captions
human_samples.recruitment: The New Yorker invites readers weekly to submit English-language captions.
human_samples.observations: Jain et al. source: roughly 250 contests from #508 onward, mean/median 6.1K/5.7K unique captions per contest, 1.5M total. Shahaf/Radev source: contests #1–#507, 2M unique captions, mean/median 5.2K/5.0K per contest. The constructed high-quality set contains 2.7K captions across 704 contests.
human_samples.detail: These are human-authored contest captions; the paper does not report the number of distinct entrants.

human_samples.recruitment: Editorial selection process of The New Yorker Caption Contest.
human_samples.observations: Editors choose three finalists from submissions for contests where official finalists are available; these finalists are automatically added to the benchmark high-quality caption set and support NYAcc labels.
human_samples.detail: The number and identities of editors are not reported.

human_samples.population: Readers/crowd raters using the NEXT platform
human_samples.recruitment: Crowd ratings gathered through the NEXT platform used by The New Yorker/Jain et al. corpus.
human_samples.observations: Over 114M ratings total, with mean/median 445K/471K ratings per contest; raters mark captions as “funny”, “somewhat funny”, or “unfunny”.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports ratings, not participant counts; the number of distinct raters is not reported.

human_samples.participant_count: 21 qualified out of 30 manually graded in the qualification round; exact number completing production annotation HITs not reported
human_samples.population: Amazon Mechanical Turk workers with 10K prior approved HITs and at least 97% acceptance rate
human_samples.recruitment: Mechanical Turk qualification/training round for description/link HITs; workers from AU, CA, NZ, GB, and US were allowed.
human_samples.geography: AU, CA, NZ, GB, US for the description/link qualification round
human_samples.observations: For 704 cartoons, the paper gathered 2 scene-location annotations, 3 literal descriptions, 3 unusual/uncanny descriptions, and 2 sets of 2–3 English Wikipedia links per cartoon. The description HIT was run 3 times per cartoon and the Wikipedia-link HIT 2 times per cartoon. Annotations were gathered in mid-to-late 2021.
human_samples.detail: Workers were paid at least $15/hour; adult-content warnings were shown. Low-resolution images received extra pay and at least one annotation by an author using the same HIT interface.

human_samples.recruitment: Internal author annotation after crowdworker explanation attempts were unsatisfactory.
human_samples.observations: The author sampled one high-quality caption per contest, reported understanding 651 of 704 sampled captions, and wrote 651 joke explanations; mean/median length 60/59 words, 39.3K words total.
human_samples.detail: These human-authored explanations serve as references and as the human side in pairwise explanation evaluations.

human_samples.population: Two authors and one person familiar with the project
human_samples.recruitment: Internal/project-familiar participants; Lisa Watkins is thanked for contributing to human performance estimates.
human_samples.observations: Each attempted 100 randomly sampled instances from both matching and quality ranking; 595 total responses were collected.
human_samples.detail: Participants saw the images but not the human-authored descriptions, corresponding to the From Pixels setting. Matching instances were sampled so there were no repeated options.

human_samples.participant_count: 29 qualified out of 51 who attempted the pairwise-judgment qualification; exact number completing final judgments not reported
human_samples.population: Amazon Mechanical Turk workers qualified for pairwise explanation judgments
human_samples.recruitment: Second Mechanical Turk qualification round about 9 months after the description/link annotations; workers rated manually selected good/bad joke explanations and were manually screened.
human_samples.observations: Pairwise explanation HITs were run 2.7K times; Table 3 reports 393, 393, 390, 396, 396, 384, and 390 ratings for Q1–Q7 respectively, with 3 crowdworkers per test instance and majority vote determining winners.
human_samples.detail: Workers were asked to choose the more complete, relevant, and convincing explanation; the interface stated that explanations may be human- or machine-written.

judges.name: New Yorker editors
judges.detail: Selected official finalists; official finalists are treated as high-quality captions and define NYAcc targets where available.

judges.name: NEXT platform crowd ratings
judges.detail: Reader ratings of captions as funny/somewhat funny/unfunny provide per-caption mean scores; top crowd-ranked captions are used as high-quality candidates and CrowdAcc targets.

judges.name: One paper author
judges.detail: Authored 651 reference joke explanations after filtering out cases the author did not understand.

judges.name: Mechanical Turk cartoon description/link annotators
judges.detail: Provided scene locations, literal descriptions, uncanny/unusual descriptions, and English Wikipedia links that are used as From Description inputs and OFA training targets.

judges.name: Mechanical Turk pairwise explanation judges
judges.detail: For explanation evaluation, 3 crowdworkers per test instance chose between unlabeled pairs of explanations; majority vote determined the winner in Table 3.

judges.name: Three human performance-estimate participants
judges.detail: Two authors and one project-familiar person answered matching and quality-ranking instances from images only; their accuracy is reported as the human estimate in Table 2.

judges.name: Text-only T5-Large quality-ranking classifier
judges.detail: For contests without direct crowd ratings, the authors trained T5-Large classifiers on crowd-labeled good/bad captions and used them to discard the 25% of entries with the lowest predicted score and help sample “okay” negative captions.

judges.name: Accuracy
judges.detail: Matching and quality-ranking results are computed as accuracy averaged over 5 cross-validation splits; quality ranking reports NYAcc and CrowdAcc depending on label source.

judges.name: BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, word-level perplexity
judges.detail: Appendix E reports automatic explanation metrics against human-written references; the paper states human evaluations are better quality measures.

judges.name: Sentence-BERT paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2 with hierarchical clustering; string deduplication
judges.detail: Used to semantically deduplicate high-quality caption candidates and “okay” caption pools during task construction.

generation_selection.scope: Evaluated explanation-generation systems for Table 3 and Appendix E
generation_selection.n: 1 evaluated explanation per model/item; no best-of-N selection reported
generation_selection.detail: T5, OFA→T5, GPT-3, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and caption-only systems are compared using a single generated explanation per instance in pairwise or automatic evaluation; repeated human voting over a fixed output is not generation selection.

generation_selection.scope: OFA-Huge image-to-annotation generation used in OFA→LM From Pixels pipeline
generation_selection.detail: For each image, OFA is queried with four annotation prompts and the top-1 prediction according to beam search with n=5 is selected.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-style multiple-choice predictions for matching and quality ranking
generation_selection.n: one response/prediction per prompt; no self-consistency or best-of-N reported
generation_selection.detail: GPT-3 uses zero-shot or 5-shot completion prompts; GPT-3.5/GPT-4 use Chat API prompts, with zero-shot chain-of-thought in some settings. Repeated sampling/selection is not reported for evaluated predictions.

generation_selection.scope: Appendix G unconditional creative caption/cartoon generation demonstration
generation_selection.detail: The paper presents an unconditional sample cherry-picked from 3 outputs using a curated brainstorming prompt.

generation_selection.scope: Appendix G conditional creative caption generation demonstration for Contest #818 scene
generation_selection.detail: The paper presents a conditional sample cherry-picked from 5 outputs using the same sampling parameters.

model_provenance.name: CLIP ViT-L/14@336px
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: ViT-L/14@336px in tables/appendix; main text contains `ViT-L/14@366px`
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned for multiple-choice matching/ranking; 428M parameters; zero-shot variant also evaluated. The paper reports resizing/padding to a 336px square input.

model_provenance.name: OFA-Huge
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned to generate four annotation types from images; composed with T5-Large/T5-11B for downstream tasks. Direct OFA fine-tuning for explanation was attempted in early experiments but was less effective.

model_provenance.name: T5-Large
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned for From Description tasks and used in OFA→T5-Large; a text-only T5-Large classifier was also trained for quality-ranking data construction.

model_provenance.name: T5-11B
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned for From Description tasks, OFA→T5-11B, and caption-only baseline.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI `davinci`
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned through the OpenAI fine-tuning API as GPT3-175B; the paper states precise API details, including which parameters are updated and which version of `davinci` is used, were not available.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI `text-davinci-002`
model_provenance.detail: Used for main GPT-3 zero-shot and 5-shot/in-context experiments and Appendix G demonstrations; described as 175B.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI `text-ada-001`
model_provenance.detail: Used in GPT-3 scaling experiments with 5 in-context examples.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI `text-babbage-001`
model_provenance.detail: Used in GPT-3 scaling experiments with 5 in-context examples.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI `text-curie-001`
model_provenance.detail: Used in GPT-3 scaling experiments with 5 in-context examples.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI `text-davinci-001`
model_provenance.detail: Used in GPT-3 scaling experiments with 5 in-context examples.

model_provenance.name: GPT-3.5
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated through OpenAI Chat API in zero-shot+CoT and 5-shot settings; exact snapshot/model ID is not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated through OpenAI Chat API in zero-shot+CoT and 5-shot settings after initial submission; exact snapshot/model ID is not reported.

model_provenance.name: Sentence-BERT `paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2`
model_provenance.detail: Used to compute embeddings for semantic deduplication and clustering of high-quality candidates and “okay” captions.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: one forward pass at test time is required after randomly sampling a single valid annotation tuple
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: At test time, one tuple is randomly selected and fixed for all comparisons; training uses all valid annotation tuples.
inference_budgets.detail: Each cartoon has 2 locations × 3 descriptions × 3 uncanny descriptions × 2 entity-link sets, but the authors construct 6 annotator-consistent tuples and sample one at test time.

inference_budgets.scope: OFA-Huge annotation generation in OFA→LM pipeline
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 4 OFA queries per image, one each for location, description, uncanny description, and entities
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: beam search n=5 with top-1 selected
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Prompts: “Where does this take place?”, “Describe this image.”, “What’s unusual about this image?”, and “What entities are there?”
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Fine-tuned on 8 A100 GPUs; maximum 7 epochs; warmup ratio 6%; learning rate 5e-5; label-smoothed cross-entropy with smoothing 0.1.
inference_budgets.detail: OFA was fine-tuned separately for each cross-validation split with validation-set early stopping.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 evaluated output per item; no alternate-candidate selection reported
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: For explanation, T5 samples with temperature 1.0 and nucleus sampling p=.95.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: T5, CLIP, and OFA were trained using 8 A100 GPUs in PyTorch. T5-11B used DeepSpeed; T5-Large used Accelerate. T5-Large learning-rate search over {1e-4, 1e-5, 5e-5}, batch size 64; T5-11B learning rate 1e-5, batch size 64.
inference_budgets.detail: Output length/token limits are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: scores multiple-choice answer options; exact forward-pass accounting not reported
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 5 choices for matching; 2 choices for quality ranking
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: No generative sampling; zero-shot prompt reported as `a new yorker cartoon with` plus the candidate caption. Fine-tuning uses image augmentations: random horizontal flipping, random color jittering, and random grayscaling.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Trained using 8 A100 GPUs; batch size 32; 200-step linear warmup; per-split learning-rate search over {5e-5, 1e-5, 5e-6}.
inference_budgets.detail: Images are resized and padded to keep the full cartoon in the 336px square input.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-3 zero-shot and 5-shot Completion API experiments
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not explicitly reported; prompts are single-instance task prompts
inference_budgets.token_budget: Figure 10 gives a randomly selected in-context explanation prompt of 1095 tokens; no global token budget is reported.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: 5 random labeled in-context examples for few-shot prompts; evaluated decoding parameters are not fully reported. Figure 10 qualitative samples use temperature .8 from different GPT-3 engines.
inference_budgets.detail: Appendix B.5 gives prompt examples for zero-shot and in-context tasks.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: For explanation fine-tuning, default settings are used; for multiple choice, `prompt_loss_weight` is set to zero. Validation is not used by the API for early stopping; train and validation sets are concatenated.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Approximately (3 tasks) × (5 cross-validation splits) × ($40 per fine-tune) = $600.
inference_budgets.detail: The precise fine-tuning API internals and `davinci` version are not available to the authors.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: not explicitly reported; prompts produce a single answer/explanation in reported evaluations
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Default hyperparameters in all cases; 5 random labeled in-context examples for few-shot; zero-shot GPT-3.5/GPT-4 use chain-of-thought prompts.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: GPT-4 queries cost on the order of $4K; GPT-3.5 is described as an order of magnitude less expensive than GPT-4.
inference_budgets.detail: Exact model snapshot IDs, token limits, and exact query dates are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Appendix G creative caption-generation demonstrations with `text-davinci-002`
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 3 unconditional outputs for the shown unconditional sample; 5 conditional outputs for the shown conditional sample
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 3 for unconditional demonstration; 5 for conditional demonstration
inference_budgets.detail: Shown outputs are explicitly cherry-picked demonstrations, not the main benchmark evaluation.

human_baselines.scope: Matching and quality ranking versus From Pixels systems
human_baselines.description: Three humans answered image-only matching and quality-ranking instances; Table 2 reports Human Estimate From Pixels: 94.0 matching accuracy, 83.7 CrowdAcc, 64.6 NYAcc.
human_baselines.detail: Humans and From Pixels models both had access to cartoon images rather than human-authored descriptions, though the paper cautions human performance is not an upper bound because labels aggregate editor/crowd preferences.

human_baselines.scope: Matching and quality ranking versus From Description systems
human_baselines.description: The same Human Estimate From Pixels is shown in Table 2 alongside From Description model results.
human_baselines.detail: From Description models receive human-authored scene annotations at test time, while the human estimate participants saw images and not the descriptions.

human_baselines.description: Human-authored explanations by one paper author are compared head-to-head against machine explanations; in Q7, human explanations beat 5-shot GPT-4 in 67.7% of pairwise cases.
human_baselines.detail: The pairwise evaluation presents unlabeled human and model explanations for the same cartoon/caption to the same crowd judges. Generation conditions are not identical because the human references were authored as a corpus by one author rather than collected as a timed participant baseline.

mitigations: Five cross-validation splits hold out entire contests for supervised fine-tuning/evaluation.
Matching distractors are balanced so each answer appears once as correct and four times as incorrect, limiting answer-only biases.
Human-authored cartoon descriptions and joke explanations were newly collected by the authors and were not described as coming from public prior benchmark data.
Quality-ranking “okay” captions are filtered/deduplicated and paired by the authors rather than using only preexisting public pairings.
contamination_risk.basis: The paper does not report a contamination or memorization analysis. Although the assembled benchmark and annotations are new, the underlying New Yorker contests, captions, finalist selections, and prior caption-contest corpora substantially predate the evaluated proprietary LLMs, and GPT-3/GPT-4 training data are opaque. Memorizing public caption-cartoon or finalist associations could help matching and NYAcc-style ranking, and possibly crowd-ranking where prior ratings/corpora were public. The risk is therefore assessed as high for headline LLM results despite cross-validation and newly created annotations.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Quality-ranking benchmark construction for contests without crowd ratings partly depends on a text-only T5-Large quality classifier used to filter low-scoring negative candidates; headline explanation-quality claims are based on human pairwise judgments, not LLM judges.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: No direct human validation of the T5 filtering decisions is reported. Positive/high-quality labels come from New Yorker editors and/or crowd ratings; explanation outputs are judged by MTurk crowdworkers.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: No LLM is used to score or rank the evaluated model outputs in the main results. However, an LLM-style T5 classifier is used as a data-construction filter, so LLM-judge dependence is secondary rather than none.

reporting_gaps: Exact number of unique MTurk workers who completed production description/link annotations is not reported; only 21 qualified out of 30 are reported.
Exact number of unique MTurk workers who completed final pairwise explanation judgments is not reported; only 29 qualified out of 51 and approximately 2.7K HITs/ratings are reported.
Participant demographics beyond limited MTurk qualification/geography for description/link workers are not reported.
Geographic eligibility for the second, pairwise-explanation MTurk study is not reported.
The reason for 595 human performance-estimate responses rather than the 600 implied by 3 people attempting 100 matching and 100 quality-ranking instances is not explained.
Exact GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 snapshot/model IDs are not reported; the paper only states May 12, 2023 versions.
Exact OpenAI API inference dates are not reported for GPT-3, GPT-3.5, or GPT-4.
The precise `davinci` version and fine-tuning internals for the OpenAI fine-tuning API are unavailable/not reported.

Token limits, maximum output lengths, and most decoding parameters for evaluated GPT-3/GPT-3.5/GPT-4 generations are not reported beyond default hyperparameters for GPT-3.5/GPT-4 and illustrative GPT-3 samples.
Number of generated candidates per evaluated OpenAI explanation is not explicitly stated; no best-of-N is reported for evaluated outputs.
Ordering/randomization details for pairwise explanation choices are not reported, although pairs are unlabeled with respect to source.
No decontamination, memorization, or training-data overlap test is reported for proprietary or pretrained models.
The paper reports training hardware for local models but not wall-clock training time or inference cost for local models.
The paper contains a CLIP input-size inconsistency: the model is written as `ViT-L/14@366px` in main text but `ViT-L/14@336px` in tables/appendix.
